entities.description: A signed two-page antiquarian contribution printing the 1463 inventory from a manuscript then in private hands. It supplies the modern title and headnote, gives the inventory in two columns, italicizes three doubtful readings, uses ellipses for damaged or incomplete kitchen entries, and closes with a note that the writer cannot be identified. It does not print the 1444 heading and material on the reverse flyleaf.
entities.label: W.'s 1852 printed transcription, Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century
notes: The contributor is known only by the printed signature W.; no full name is supplied.
Dutschke describes the article as printed from HM 744 while it was privately held and notes its omission of the heading on f. iv.

rights.basis: The contribution was published in 1852 and is outside copyright term in both jurisdictions.
rights.component: The 1852 printed transcription, title, headnote, tailnote, and typography

dateStatements.label: Principal codex production
dateStatements.notes: This broad catalog date does not date the later inventory addition.

entities.description: The physical composite codex now called HM 744. The target household inventory is a later addition in another hand on the recto of the rear flyleaf, f. ir, dated 15 May 1463. The verso, f. iv, bears different inventory material and a possibly erroneous 1444 date in the same hand; it is not part of CME00123.

holding.notes: At the 1852 publication the owner was not named beyond 'private hands.' Recorded provenance then identifies the Earl of Ashburnham's Appendix 133; the Appendix manuscripts passed to Henry Yates Thompson in 1897, this item sold through Leighton in 1899, then belonged to George Dunn, passed through Maggs, and was owned by Sir Israel Gollancz. Henry E. Huntington acquired it from A. S. W. Rosenbach in June 1925. The evidence does not establish whether Ashburnham already owned it when the article appeared.

entities.label: San Marino, Huntington Library, HM 744, f. ir

physicalDescription.extent: 68 leaves; target addition on rear flyleaf f. ir
physicalDescription.format: Bound composite manuscript with later flyleaf additions
notes: The principal codex contains didactic and literary pieces, including Hoccleve material; the household inventory is a separate flyleaf addition in another hand.
The target leaf's outer edge is badly deteriorated, and reagent treatment made portions difficult or nearly impossible to read.

Dutschke says bracket-supplied words in the catalog description are no longer present; the 1852 transcription may therefore preserve readings no longer legible, but the exact chronology of loss is not known.
The f. ir inventory and f. iv inventory material are in the same hand but bear different dates, 1463 and 1444 respectively.
Recorded modern provenance: Earl of Ashburnham, Appendix 133; Henry Yates Thompson; Leighton; George Dunn; Maggs; Sir Israel Gollancz; A. S. W. Rosenbach; Henry E. Huntington, 1925.

rights.basis: The text was created in 1463 and its author is anonymous; any copyright in the medieval composition has long expired.
rights.component: The anonymous Middle English inventory text created in 1463

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00123.xml. The anonymous 1463 inventory, the 1852 printed contribution, U-M encoding, physical codex, facsimiles, and catalog description are modeled separately.
The current witness is Huntington HM 744, not HM 114. Dutschke explicitly identifies the 1852 Retrospective Review text as printed from HM 744 when the codex was privately held.

The target work occupies HM 744 f. ir alone. F. iv contains a separate heading and inventory material dated 1444 in the same hand; it is retained as neighboring context in the complete facsimile bundle but excluded from the transcription relation's target scope.
The Huntington catalog's broad 1425-1450 date describes the principal codex. The inventory addition on the rear flyleaf explicitly dates itself 15 May 1463 and is modeled at component level.

The article appeared in the November 1852 installment, as shown by its running header and the volume's installment history. The collected volume is dated 1853, so both dates occur in legitimate catalog citations.

The 1852 print preserves three doubtful readings in italics and uses ellipses for incomplete kitchen entries. Dutschke reports severe outer-edge deterioration and reagent treatment, and says several bracket-supplied words in his description are no longer present.
The printed article is signed only 'W.' No reliable evidence was found for expanding that initial to Thomas Wright.

Sutton links HM 744, a merchant mark, Thomas Fyler, and property at Little Baddow; LMES instead says the flyleaf inventories were probably by or for John Fyler. Neither claim overrides the manuscript's anonymous status.

The XML contains sixty-nine ITEM nodes, but one is administrative content inside TEMPHEAD. The intellectual text contains sixty-eight body ITEM nodes: nine top-level room or space entries and fifty-nine nested list entries, not sixty-eight discrete physical objects.

The IIIF canvas identifier published inside the legacy manifest does not currently dereference directly. Exact Image API request URLs are therefore used for the work boundary, while the manifest URL remains the bundle source.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan CME00123, an encoding of the complete signed two-page contribution 'Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century' in the November 1852 installment of The Retrospective Review, new series volume 1, pp. 101-102; the collected-volume title page is dated 1853. The contribution transcribes the anonymous household inventory dated 15 May 1463 on Huntington Library HM 744, rear flyleaf f. ir, but omits the different 1444 inventory material on f. iv. The article named no private owner; recorded provenance includes the Earl of Ashburnham's Appendix collection and a documented chain ending in Huntington's 1925 acquisition. A complete 451-page scan of the printed volume and all 153 images in Huntington's provider-published IIIF sequence are cached locally with fixity. The work-specific manuscript boundary is f. ir, canvas 145, images/000145.jpg; the full-manuscript cache deliberately retains the adjacent f. iv context without treating it as part of CME00123.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.title: Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century

catalogingSubject.label: Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous first-person household inventory dated 15 May 1463 and written at a place called 'litill Barw', identified by catalog and contextual scholarship as Little Baddow, Essex. It is not the whole Huntington HM 744 codex, the different 1444 inventory material on f. iv, the 1852 journal article as a bibliographic carrier, the University of Michigan encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact. CME00123 retains the 1852 editor's supplied title, headnote, tailnote, and signature as distinct paratext.

contentParts.label: Editorial title and headnote
notes: Paratext supplied by the nineteenth-century contribution, not medieval title matter.
contentParts.summary: The supplied title and note that the source was then a manuscript in private hands.

contentParts.label: Household inventory dated 15 May 1463
notes: The separate 1444 material on f. iv is outside this work boundary.
contentParts.summary: First-person preamble and room-by-room list of household goods.

contentParts.label: Editorial tailnote and signature
contentParts.summary: An editor's statement that the original did not identify its writer, followed by the signature 'W.'

assertion.notes: The inventory itself says it was written with the writer's own hand on this date.
The dated inventory survives as an addition on HM 744 f. ir; the codex's broader catalog date of 1425-1450 belongs to its principal medieval contents, not this later flyleaf addition.
The article appeared in the November 1852 installment. The collected volume title page is dated 1853, explaining catalog citations to new series volume 1 (1853).

extent.description: One prose inventory in one DIV1, with one preamble paragraph and sixty-eight body ITEM elements arranged as nine top-level space entries and fifty-nine nested entries; plus editorial title, headnote, tailnote, and signature.

formStatements.description: A first-person prose preamble followed by nested room-by-room lists of household goods.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English title, headnote, and tailnote are editorial paratext and are not promoted to a second intellectual-work language facet.
The 1852 article is the immediate source of the U-M encoding; the current manuscript shelfmark is Huntington HM 744, not HM 114.
The collected Retrospective Review volume is dated 1853, but the article's running headers and installment chronology date its issue to November 1852.
The Huntington catalog's broad 1425-1450 date describes the codex's principal contents and must not replace the inventory's explicit date of 15 May 1463.
The complete HM 744 digital sequence is cached locally; the work-specific locus is f. ir/canvas 145, while f. iv/canvas 146 contains separate 1444 material.

next_steps: Compare the inventory hand and merchant marks against identified Fyler-family entries throughout HM 744.
Consult specialist paleographic work or request a Huntington curatorial opinion before strengthening any attribution.
notes: The present manifest keeps the author anonymous and treats Thomas Fyler only as a low-confidence attribution.
openQuestions.question: Was the 1463 inventory written by Thomas Fyler, John Fyler, another Fyler-family member, or an unidentified householder?

next_steps: Search publisher correspondence, contributor indexes, and contemporary journal notices for the new-series Retrospective Review.
notes: No reliable evidence was found for expanding W. to Thomas Wright.
openQuestions.question: Who was the contributor or transcriber signing the 1852 article as 'W.'?

next_steps: Compare the high-resolution provider image line by line with the 1852 print and XML.
Ask Huntington whether higher-resolution or multispectral imaging exists for the reagent-darkened flyleaf.
notes: Dutschke marks several supplied words as no longer present and reports damage and reagent treatment; the print may preserve readings no longer legible, but the exact state at transcription is not established.
openQuestions.question: Which readings and layout details on damaged HM 744 f. ir can now be recovered independently of the 1852 transcription?

assertion.notes: The text locates the listed possessions in the writer's place at a damaged toponym; Little Baddow is a strong contextual identification, but this is not promoted to a certain place of composition.
The first-person statement establishes self-inscription but not a personal name.

Sutton's contextual case connects Thomas Fyler with HM 744 and Little Baddow, but the Huntington catalog does not name the inventory writer; LMES instead says the inventories were probably by or for John Fyler.
The article is signed W.; that signature supports responsibility for the printed contribution but does not disclose a full identity or distinguish transcription from editorial preparation.

scope.description: The 1852 printed transcription and its editorial framing

summaries.value: A household inventory dated 15 May 1463, introduced as a list of goods at the writer's place in 'litill Barw', probably Little Baddow, Essex. It moves through hall, parlour, buttery, chamber, kitchen, outbuildings, stable, cheese house, and barn, recording furniture, wall hangings, tableware, linens, bedding, tools, vessels, tack, and farm equipment.

CME00123 represents the complete two-page 1852 article: its supplied title and private-manuscript headnote, the 1463 inventory, the editorial statement that the writer cannot be identified, and the signature 'W.' It does not encode the different 1444 heading and inventory material on HM 744 f. iv or surrounding journal contributions.

The XML contains sixty-nine ITEM elements, but one is administrative prose inside TEMPHEAD. The represented article therefore has sixty-eight body ITEM elements: nine top-level room or space entries and fifty-nine nested inventory entries. These XML list items are not sixty-eight discrete physical objects because several entries aggregate multiple goods and quantities.

assertion.notes: This established access title is editorial and does not appear as a medieval title on HM 744 f. ir.

titles.supplied_by: The unidentified editor signing as W. in The Retrospective Review
titles.value: Household Inventory of the Fifteenth Century

assertion.notes: A descriptive supplied title clarifying date and place; 'litill Barw' is damaged in the witness and expanded as Little Baddow by catalog and contextual evidence.

titles.value: Household Inventory of 1463 at Little Baddow
Thes parcelles her after wretyn ben in my place in litill Barw

work.summary: A household inventory dated 15 May 1463, introduced as a list of goods at the writer's place in 'litill Barw', probably Little Baddow, Essex. It moves through hall, parlour, buttery, chamber, kitchen, outbuildings, stable, cheese house, and barn, recording furniture, wall hangings, tableware, linens, bedding, tools, vessels, tack, and farm equipment.
